lens.included_activities: No qualifying lower-middle-market outsourced-service firms were identified; the correctly classified US population consists of government legislative establishments and their advisory committees and commissions.
lens.excluded_activities: Congress, state and local legislative bodies, advisory and study legislative commissions, captive government offices, political campaigns, private lobbying and government-relations firms, govtech vendors, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Legislative bodies exercise public authority and are funded through governmental appropriations rather than recurring fees paid by external commercial customers; constituents are represented citizens, not customers under an outsourced-service contract.

a.bridge: O*NET describes elected legislators rather than the full wage-weighted occupation mix of legislative establishments, while NCSL surveys legislative staff rather than measuring task-time exposure. I map research, summarization, first drafts, editing, transcription, data comparison, bill and report preparation, constituent-message triage, and routine administration to AI exposure, while treating voting, democratic judgment, negotiation, hearings, public representation, relationship-building, supervision, and final accountability as less substitutable. Staff-focused evidence may overstate exposure for elected officials but understate the staff share of establishment wages.
a.rationale: Legislative work has a large language, research, document, and information-processing layer that current generative AI can assist. The core exercise of public authority remains human and institutionally constrained, and no wage-weighted staffing or task-time study for NAICS 921120 was found.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-linked time-use data from federal, state, and local legislative offices separating elected officials, counsel, analysts, clerks, communications, constituent services, and administration, paired with audited AI task trials, would materially change the range.
caveats: O*NET includes only elected legislators and omits most legislative staff occupations.
NCSL's survey is a small, non-wage-weighted staff sample and reports uses, not avoidable labor share.
The frozen labor-share input is missing because government activity has no comparable receipts denominator.

rho.bridge: NCSL reports that 44 percent of 2025 survey respondents were already using generative AI for legislative work, a current adoption measure rather than the five-year implemented share of the exposed labor opportunity. I use observed adoption and the absence of blanket prohibitions in that sample to anchor implementation above a purely experimental level, then adjust upward over five years while discounting for confidentiality, accuracy, approved-tool, permission, procurement, records, and human-review constraints. Adoption can overstate effective labor substitution because casual use may save little time.
rho.rationale: Implementation should expand through approved research, drafting, transcription, translation, data, and communication workflows, but legislative offices require reviewable sources, careful handling of sensitive information, accessibility, public-record compliance, and accountable human sign-off. Political and institutional variation keeps the range wide.
rho.change_evidence: A repeated NCSL census with response weights, office-level policies, production usage logs, error and correction rates, time saved, staffing effects, and procurement status by legislature would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Current tool use is not equivalent to sustained workflow implementation or avoided hiring.
The 68 responses span 35 states, Puerto Rico, and the US Virgin Islands but are not a probability sample of all legislative bodies.
Implementation excludes demand, pricing, and valuation effects.

e.rationale: The denominator required by e does not exist for the frozen lens: NAICS 921120 comprises government establishments, while the lens requires transferable LMM firms supplying outsourced services. Assigning zero would turn an undefined share into a measured structural value.
e.change_evidence: A scope change that explicitly admits privatized legislative operators, or firm-level evidence that commercial contract entities are correctly classified inside NAICS 921120, would be required before e could be defined.
caveats: The official definition identifies government establishments rather than private firms.
Govtech, lobbying, and government-relations vendors are outside this NAICS code and the frozen lens.
The frozen LMM firm-count input is also missing.

s5.rationale: There is no eligible firm population for a qualifying control transfer. Elections, appointments, reorganizations, and jurisdictional changes are changes in public governance, not acquisitions of transferable commercial operations.
s5.change_evidence: Evidence of correctly classified, privately controlled legislative-service firms in the LMM band and a transaction history for those firms would be required to define s5.
caveats: Political turnover is not a control transaction under the screen.
Government reorganizations do not create transferable equity ownership.
A probability conditional on an empty eligible population is undefined.

q.rationale: Legislative bodies do not bill external customers under commercial contracts, so there is no defensible operator-retention share after customer pass-through or renewal repricing. Budget savings, appropriations, and public-value improvements are not equivalent to commercial gross-benefit retention.
q.change_evidence: A revised scope covering a fee-charging outsourced legislative-service provider, together with its contracts, pricing, renewals, and realized post-implementation margins, would be required to define q.
caveats: Appropriation outcomes and budget clawbacks are not customer pricing mechanisms.
No eligible commercial operator was identified.
Using a generic public-sector savings-retention assumption would not answer the primitive.

d5.bridge: The Census Bureau reports 90,837 local governments in 2022 and 91,438 in 2025, but total local-government units are not legislative-body service quantity and much of the increase came from special districts. I use the broadly stable institutional count as evidence that the underlying public-governance workload is unlikely to disappear, while keeping the five-year constant-quality demand range close to flat. The proxy may overstate growth because many special districts lack separately classified legislative establishments.
d5.rationale: Federal and state legislatures are institutionally fixed, and local government counts changed little in aggregate. Population, policy complexity, constituent communication, and oversight can increase workload, while consolidation and process automation can offset it; the current service basket is therefore expected to remain approximately stable.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year panel of QCEW employment and hours for NAICS 921120, counts of separately staffed legislative establishments, bill and amendment volume, hearing hours, constituent casework, and jurisdiction consolidations would materially change the ratio.
caveats: Government-unit counts are a stock proxy, not constant-price legislative-service quantity.
Special districts drove much of the reported increase and may not map to this NAICS code.
Federal, state, county, and municipal legislative workloads differ substantially.

o.rationale: AI can assist inputs to legislation, but enacted law, votes, hearings, negotiation, representation, and public accountability remain functions of authorized human institutions. Within five years, software may reduce staffing needs and enable more self-service information access without eliminating the accountable legislative operator.
o.change_evidence: Statutory authorization of autonomous legislative decisions, sustained substitution of hearings and constituent representation by software, or elimination and consolidation data for legislative bodies would materially lower this value.
caveats: The estimate concerns operator requirement, not internal labor exposure.
Some information and constituent-service tasks may shift to self-service portals without eliminating the legislature.
Institutional and constitutional requirements differ across jurisdictions.

narrative.executive_view: Legislative bodies have a substantial AI-assistable research and document workload, and adoption is already visible among legislative staff. They are government institutions rather than transferable outsourced-service firms, however, so the commercial acquisition lens has no defensible eligible population or value-capture construct.
narrative.ai_impact: Generative AI is already used for first drafts, email, data comparison, transcription, research, reports, resolutions, and bill summaries. It can reduce staff effort in these workflows, but source verification, confidentiality, public records, legal precision, democratic judgment, negotiation, voting, and final accountability require strong human control.
narrative.value_capture: Commercial retention is undefined because legislative bodies receive appropriations rather than customer fees. Efficiency can create fiscal capacity or budget savings, but those outcomes cannot be translated honestly into contract pass-through, renewal repricing, or operator margin.
narrative.firm_availability: The official industry population consists of government legislative establishments and commissions. Elections and reorganizations do not constitute control transfers, the frozen LMM firm count is missing, and private vendors serving legislatures belong in other industries.
narrative.demand_durability: The number of US local governments was broadly stable from 2022 to 2025, while federal and state legislative institutions persist by law. Workload may shift with policy complexity and digital channels, but an accountable legislature remains necessary even if staffing and workflow change.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest analytical limitation is the empty investment lens. For operational AI, uncertainty centers on the unobserved staff occupation mix, small survey samples, accuracy and citation failures, confidential information, records and procurement rules, political variation, and the distinction between tool use and realized labor avoidance.
narrative.principal_driver: Research, drafting, summarization, transcription, and constituent-information workflows create a meaningful implementable AI assistance opportunity inside legislative operations.
narrative.principal_weakness: Government legislative establishments are neither transferable LMM firms nor commercial outsourced-service operators, leaving eligibility, transfer, and retention undefined.

sources.title: NAICS 921120 Legislative Bodies
sources.supports: Defines the industry as government establishments serving as legislative bodies and their advisory committees and commissions; supports the lens and the absence of a commercial firm population.

sources.title: 11-1031.00 - Legislators
sources.supports: Defines elected legislators and lists analysis, negotiation, drafting, hearings, constituent review, voting, public representation, oversight, and office-management tasks; supports a and o reasoning.

sources.title: Legislative Use of Artificial Intelligence 2025 Survey
sources.supports: Reports legislative-staff AI adoption, use cases, and policy constraints from 68 responses across 35 states and three territories; supports a and rho.

sources.title: Government Organization Summary Report: 2022 & 2025
sources.supports: Reports total US local-government counts in 2022 and 2025 and identifies the source of most growth; supports the d5 proxy.
